prompt: Explain the attention mechanism in transformer models to a software engineer who has never done ML. Keep it to about 200 words.
response:
Attention in transformers is a fast, parallel way for the model to let every item in a sequence “focus” on other items when producing a representation. Think of each token as emitting three vectors: a query (Q), a key (K) and a value (V). The model computes how much each query should attend to each key by measuring their similarity, converts those similarities into normalized weights, and uses the weights to mix the values into a new representation for each token.

Concretely: compute scores = Q × K^T, scale them (divide by sqrt(d)), apply softmax across each query’s scores to get attention weights, then output = weights × V. All Q/K/V are learned linear projections of token embeddings, so the model learns what patterns to match.

Because this is done for every token against every other, a token can directly incorporate information from distant parts of the sequence (no step-by-step recurrence). Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different projections so the model can capture multiple relation types simultaneously. Positional information is added separately because plain attention doesn’t encode order. The whole mechanism is differentiable and trained end-to-end.
